subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Core box contents listed in the rulebook: 7 Wonder boards, 7 Wonder cards, 49 Age I cards, 49 Age II cards, 50 Age III cards, 46 Conflict tokens, 24 coins of value 3, 46 coins of value 1, 1 score booklet, 1 rulebook, and 2 cards for 2-player mode.

Important deck composition note from setup rules: Age III includes 10 Guild cards (purple), but not all are used each game. You keep a number of guilds equal to player count + 2, then shuffle those into the rest of Age III.

Card families used in scoring and play: Raw Materials (brown), Manufactured Goods (gray), Civilian Structures (blue), Scientific Structures (green), Commercial Structures (yellow), Military Structures (red), and Guilds (purple).

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1) Place coin bank and conflict token reserve in reach of all players.
2) Prepare each Age deck for the exact player count by removing cards marked above your player number. Example from rules: in a 6-player game, use 3+, 4+, 5+, and 6+ cards; remove 7+ cards.
3) For Age III only, remove all 10 guilds, randomly keep only N+2 guilds (N = number of players), and shuffle those kept guilds into the Age III deck.
4) Shuffle Wonder cards and deal one face down to each player, or mutually choose Wonder boards if all players agree. The dealt wonder card determines which board and side are used.
5) Each player places their Wonder board in front of them with their chosen side visible.

There is no central map board in 7 Wonders; each player’s city tableau forms the play area, and all card play is simultaneous.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Each player begins with exactly 3 coins of value 1 on their Wonder board area. Remaining coins form the bank; players can make change between value-1 and value-3 coins as needed.

Each player’s Wonder board grants a starting resource shown in the upper-left icon. Wonders are double-sided; side A is recommended for early teaching games because it is simpler.

At the start of each Age, every player receives a fresh hand of 7 cards from that Age’s prepared deck. Hands are hidden and chosen simultaneously each turn.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
Age I begins after all players have their 7-card hand and 3 starting coins. There is no separate initiative phase required; all players choose and reveal actions simultaneously each turn.

Ages proceed in this order: Age I, then Age II, then Age III. Each age has exactly 6 played turns. On each turn, every player chooses 1 card, resolves 1 action, then passes the remaining hand in the age-specific direction:
- Age I: pass left
- Age II: pass right
- Age III: pass left

On the 6th turn of each age, players receive only 2 cards, play 1, and discard the other without gaining 3 coins for that discarded card.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each turn in an Age is resolved in numbered order:

1) Choose a card
Every player selects one card from hand and places it face down.

2) Resolve one action simultaneously
After all players have chosen, everyone resolves exactly one of three actions with the selected card:
- Build the structure
- Build a Wonder stage
- Discard the card for 3 coins

3) Pass hands
Players pass all remaining cards to neighbor according to age direction (Age I left, Age II right, Age III left), and receive a new hand from the opposite side.

4) Repeat until 6 turns are completed in that Age
At turn 6, from a 2-card hand, choose 1 to play and discard the unchosen card face down without coin gain.

5) End of Age: Military conflict resolution
Each player compares military strength with immediate left and right neighbors and takes conflict tokens for each comparison. Then next Age begins with fresh 7-card deal.

After Age III conflict resolution, proceed directly to final scoring.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Action A — Build the selected structure card
- Pay its listed cost (if any), then place it in your city.
- You cannot build two identical structures (same name/illustration) in your city.
- Brown and gray cards are usually arranged so production is easy to read; other colors are arranged in front of your Wonder board.
- Some cards are free (empty cost box).
- Some cards cost coins paid to bank.
- Some cards cost resources and/or coins.

Resource production and use
- Resources come from your Wonder board, brown cards, gray cards, and some yellow cards.
- Resources are NOT consumed when used; production persists every turn for the whole game.
- You may use produced resources for your own build even if you sold those resources to a neighbor in the same turn.

Commerce
- You may buy missing resources only from your immediate left and/or right neighbors.
- Purchasable resources are those from their Wonder starting resource, brown cards, and gray cards.
- Certain yellow-card or wonder-produced resources may be personal-only and not purchasable.
- Default cost is 2 coins per resource bought, paid to the neighbor providing it.
- Some yellow cards reduce this buy cost from 2 to 1 for specific resource types or directions.
- Neighbors cannot refuse to sell.
- Bought resources can only be used on that turn.
- You must have enough coins at the START of your turn to buy; coins gained during the same turn cannot be immediately reused for purchases that turn.

Action B — Build a Wonder stage
- Instead of building your selected card, place it face down under your Wonder board as a construction marker for the next stage.
- Pay the stage cost printed on your Wonder board (ignore the selected card’s own cost).
- Gain that Wonder stage’s reward immediately.
- Wonder stages must be built in order from left to right.

Action C — Discard selected card for money
- Discard selected card to gain 3 coins from the bank.
- Exception on final turn of each Age: the unchosen card from your 2-card hand is discarded with NO 3-coin gain.

Free construction chains
- Some Age II and Age III cards can be built for free if you built the prerequisite chain symbol/name in a prior age (shown on the card).
- If chain condition is met, ignore normal resource cost for that card.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Simultaneous drafting engine
All players choose and resolve at once, then pass hands. You are not only building your city—you are also deciding what your neighbors will see next. Denial drafting is therefore a real strategic tool.

Military conflicts (end of each Age)
You compare total shields with each adjacent neighbor separately:
- If stronger, gain a Victory token (Age I: +1, Age II: +3, Age III: +5).
- If weaker, take a Defeat token worth -1.
- Ties generally award neither victory nor defeat token between tied players.
This means every age produces up to two military outcomes per player (left side and right side).

Card color systems
- Brown/Gray: economy foundation and production consistency.
- Yellow: commerce modifications, coin flow, and sometimes VP.
- Green: scientific scoring set-building.
- Blue: direct VP.
- Red: military strength for conflict tokens.
- Purple (Age III guilds): conditional scoring tied to table state.

Guild scaling by player count
Only N+2 guilds enter Age III each game, which changes late-game scoring texture each session and increases replayability.

Coin economy timing rule (easy to miss)
Money earned from selling resources during a turn cannot fund purchases in that same turn; it is available on following turns only. This timing rule can make otherwise legal builds impossible if you began the turn with insufficient coins.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
END TRIGGER
The game ends immediately after Age III is completed and Age III military conflicts are resolved.

FINAL SCORING
Total victory points are counted from all sources in your completed city and token area:
1) Military conflict tokens
   - Add positive victory tokens (+1/+3/+5 by age)
   - Subtract defeat tokens (-1 each)
2) Coins
   - Remaining money converts at 3 coins = 1 victory point (rounded down).
3) Civilian structures (blue cards)
   - Add printed VP.
4) Scientific structures (green cards)
   - Score based on your development in the three scientific fields per science scoring system.
5) Commercial structures (yellow cards)
   - Add any VP granted by yellow card effects.
6) Guilds (purple cards)
   - Add points according to each guild’s specific criteria.
7) Wonder stages
   - Add any VP and effects from completed Wonder stages.

WINNER
Highest total VP wins.

TIEBREAKER
If tied on VP, player with the most coins wins. If still tied, treat as shared victory unless a house rule specifies otherwise.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build a resource base in Age I first, points second.
Most beginner losses come from weak production that forces expensive buying in Ages II-III. Aim for at least one dependable raw material line and one manufactured-good path if possible.

2) Keep 2-4 coins in reserve while developing.
Even if your tableau is productive, commerce flexibility matters when draft hands are awkward. Running out of coins can strand powerful plays.

3) Read both neighbors early.
Because military compares only with adjacent cities and trade is only with adjacent cities, neighbor boards define your costs and conflict outcomes. Track what they are producing and whether they are pushing red military.

4) Build toward at least one chain.
A single free chain into Age II can save critical resources and tempo. If you can line up two chain opportunities without sacrificing economy, even better.

5) Use wonder stages as conversion turns, not default turns.
Wonder building is excellent when your current draft hand is weak for direct structures, but avoid burying high-value cards when your city can support them efficiently.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Over-prioritizing blue points in Age I.
Early points feel safe, but weak production later creates dead turns and expensive purchases that erase the advantage.

2) Forgetting that bought resources are turn-limited.
Players sometimes mentally 'bank' purchased resources; you cannot. Purchases only support that immediate build.

3) Ignoring the coin timing rule.
You cannot spend coins earned from selling this turn until future turns. This causes frequent illegal build attempts by newer players.

4) Underestimating military defeats.
Repeated -1 tokens from both neighbors across ages can quietly swing the game. Even a modest military investment can prevent that bleed.

5) Building duplicate-named structures by mistake.
Rule prohibits identical structures in one city. Verify card names before committing.

6) Drafting only for personal gain with no denial awareness.
Passing a perfect chain card or guild trigger to a neighbor can cost more than taking a slightly weaker card for yourself.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Economy depth vs immediate VP
Do you spend early turns on resource and commerce infrastructure, or lock in immediate points? In most tables, stronger economy wins unless a clear point engine is already online.

Decision 2: Wonder stage timing vs structure timing
Every wonder stage consumes a drafted card. If the card is low-value for your city, converting it into a stage is efficient. If the card would meaningfully improve your engine or deny an opponent, burying it can be costly.

Decision 3: Military thresholding
You rarely need to dominate military globally; you need to beat or tie neighbors often enough. Decide whether to invest just enough shields for parity, or push hard for multiple age wins.

Decision 4: Science commitment level
Science can score explosively, but partial science without enough symbols or chain support underperforms. Either commit with support, or treat green as opportunistic rather than primary plan.

Decision 5: Coin liquidity vs card quality
Sometimes discarding for 3 coins is the strongest play if it enables two future premium builds. Beginners often avoid discarding because it feels passive, but in 7 Wonders liquidity is tempo.

metadata.notes: Written from provided 7 Wonders rulebook text excerpt as primary source.
